Collective Intelligence
meets Artificial Intelligence.

Beyond Thinking NGO explores how people and AI can be connected so that — collectively — they act more intelligently than any individual, group, or computer has ever done before. Built on 32+ years of research by the 1492 Collective Intelligence Group, in alignment with the MIT Center for Collective Intelligence.

Beyond Thinking NGO

About Us

We explore how people and artificial intelligence can be connected so that — collectively — they act more intelligently than any individual, group, or machine has ever done before.

When knowledge is shared intentionally and structured with intelligence, the result is not 1+1=2. It is 1+1=11 — and beyond. This is the founding principle of Beyond Thinking NGO: collective intelligence, amplified by artificial intelligence, creates exponential outcomes that no individual can achieve alone.

For over 32 years, the 1492 Collective Intelligence Group has studied swarm behavior and collective intelligence at a global level, inspiring leading organizations, institutions, and governments to apply these principles through AI- and CI-powered tools, research, and transformative advisory.

Beyond Thinking NGO channels this accumulated knowledge into a global mission: advancing collective intelligence research, building high-impact networks, and proving that the wisdom of the many exponentially outperforms the knowledge of the one.

Our work is informed by deep alignment with the MIT Center for Collective Intelligence in Boston — sharing the same fundamental research question: how can people and computers be connected so that, collectively, they act more intelligently than ever before?

1 + 1 = 11
The Collective Intelligence Principle
Collective intelligence and collective consciousness — to explore the best possible outcomes for all life on this planet. — Katja Bellinghausen-Hengl, Co-Founder

Explorers Growth — The Power of Collective Intelligence
Collective Intelligence in Practice

How We Think

Albert Einstein is perhaps the most compelling historical proof of collective intelligence: his deliberate network of scientists multiplied his individual genius many times over.

Albert Einstein, 1879–1955

Network of a Genius

Despite his reputation as a solitary thinker, Einstein was a masterful practitioner of collective intelligence. He deliberately built a remarkable network of scientists and thinkers, understanding that structured intellectual exchange would expand his theories far beyond what any individual mind could achieve alone.

With Max Planck, he explored the nature of light and energy. Marcel Grossmann provided the mathematical architecture for the general theory of relativity. Rigorous debates with Niels Bohr drove fundamental advances in quantum mechanics. Marie Curie, Erwin Schrödinger, and Satyendra Nath Bose completed a network of extraordinary collective excellence.

Einstein's approach to collective intelligence reshaped modern science. He proved that revolutionary breakthroughs emerge not from isolation, but from structured collaboration, disciplined debate, and the deliberate exchange of ideas across disciplines.

At Beyond Thinking, we apply this same principle globally — connecting people and AI so that the collective always outperforms the individual.

Albert Einstein
Relativity Theory
Max Planck
Quantum Physics
Marie Curie
Radioactivity
Niels Bohr
Quantum Mechanics
Erwin Schrödinger
Wave Mechanics

Statistical Mechanics
Marcel Grossmann
Mathematics
We aspire to enrich the world using a strategy that mirrors Einstein and his network of scientists. By fostering intellectual exchange and harnessing collective intelligence — augmented by artificial intelligence — we drive innovation, empower individuals, and advance both scientific and societal progress toward a better future. — 1492 Collective Intelligence Group
Solutions

What We Do

We harness the power of collective wisdom to transform organizations, communities, and ecosystems worldwide. By connecting science, technology, culture, and leadership, we unlock exponential potential through proven collective intelligence methodologies.

01
Keynote
Inspiring talks on collective intelligence, swarm behavior, and AI-augmented organizational transformation for global conferences and leadership forums.
02
Health Check
Organizational diagnostics that identify untapped collective potential and structural barriers to intelligent collaboration.
03
Team Booster
Activating team synergy through structured CI methodologies, facilitated workshops, and AI-supported group dynamics.
04
Target Vision
Collaborative goal definition and strategic alignment for sustainable, high-impact organizational direction.
05
Coaching
Individual and group coaching for leaders and teams navigating complexity through collective and artificial intelligence.
06
Crowd Strategy
Crowdsourcing strategies that unlock distributed knowledge and collective resources at organizational and global scale.
07
Network
Building high-impact networks and strategic connections across international research, business, and innovation ecosystems.
08
Cost Intelligence
Collective optimization strategies for sustainable cost management and resource allocation across complex organizations.
09
Library  · Coming Soon
A curated global knowledge base on collective intelligence — open to members, partners, and collaborators worldwide.

Gemeinnützig­keit
Beyond Thinking NGO verfolgt ausschließlich gemeinnützige, nicht kommerzielle Zwecke im Sinne der §§ 34 ff. BAO (Bundesabgabenordnung). Der Verein ist als gemeinnützig eingetragen (ZVR 1647187653).

Angaben zur Spendenbegünstigung gemäß § 4a EStG werden nach behördlicher Anerkennung ergänzt.
Vereins­statuten
Statuten des Vereins “Beyond Thinking” gem. Vereinsgesetz 2002.
Eingetragen: 12.11.2020 · ZVR 1647187653 · BH Liezen
BEYONDTHINKING — GEMEINNÜTZIGER EINGETRAGENER VEREIN
Articles of Association · Vereinsstatuten · Registered 12 November 2020
§ 1  Name and Registered Office
  • The association bears the name "Beyond Thinking" and has its registered office at Lupitsch 123, 8992 Altaussee.
  • Its area of activity extends across Austria; the financial year is the calendar year.
  • All function titles in these statutes apply equally to all genders.
§ 2  Purpose of the Association
  • The purpose of the association is to collect, research, and disseminate new ideas, innovative thoughts, and groundbreaking concepts from art, science, and business in order to promote the integral collective intelligence of people and social systems.
  • The activities of the association are not profit-oriented.
  • The association pursues exclusively and directly charitable purposes within the meaning of §§ 34–47 of the Federal Tax Code (Bundesabgabenordnung — BAO) and is therefore a non-profit association.
§ 3  Means of Achieving the Association's Purpose
  • The purpose shall be achieved through ideal means including: donations, sponsoring, events, and publications.
  • The association is entitled to participate in (non-)profit companies, engage fulfilment agents pursuant to § 40 para. 1 BAO, or act as a fulfilment agent itself.
  • Funds may be forwarded to donation-privileged organisations pursuant to § 40a Z 1 BAO, provided at least one common organisational purpose exists.
  • Services may be rendered at cost to other non-profit or charitable organisations pursuant to § 40a Z 2 BAO.
§ 4  Types of Membership
  • Ordinary members actively participate in the association's activities.
  • Extraordinary members support the association's purpose primarily through financial contributions.
  • Honorary members are persons who have rendered special services to the association.
§ 5  Acquisition of Membership
  • Ordinary and extraordinary members may be natural or legal persons.
  • The board decides on admission of ordinary and extraordinary members. Admission may be refused without stating reasons.
  • Honorary membership is conferred by the general assembly on the proposal of the board.
§ 6  Membership Rights and Duties
  • Members have the right to participate in all events and to use all facilities of the association.
  • Only ordinary members have the right to vote in the general assembly.
  • Members are obliged to promote the interests of the association and to refrain from any action detrimental to its reputation or purpose.
  • Members must pay the membership fees set by the general assembly in the amounts and on the dates specified.
  • Admission as a member is communicated to the candidate. Honorary membership is decided unanimously by the board.
§ 7  Termination of Membership
  • Membership ceases upon death (loss of legal personality for legal persons), resignation, removal from the list, or exclusion.
  • Resignation must be notified to the board in writing at least two weeks before the end of the calendar year.
  • Removal from the membership list is permissible if a member is in arrears with payment obligations for more than four months despite two written reminders.
  • Exclusion may be resolved by the board at any time for important cause, in particular for gross breach of membership duties or conduct damaging to the association.
  • The affected member must be given the opportunity to comment before exclusion. The decision must be communicated in writing with reasons.
  • The excluded member may appeal to the internal arbitration tribunal (§ 16).
§ 8  Association Bodies
  • The bodies of the association are: the General Assembly (§§ 9, 10, 11), the Board (§§ 12, 13), the Auditors (§ 14), and the Arbitration Tribunal (§§ 15, 16).
§ 9  General Assembly
  • The general assembly is the "supreme body" of the association.
  • An ordinary general assembly takes place every two years.
  • An extraordinary general assembly must be convened within four weeks upon resolution of the board, resolution of the ordinary general assembly, written request of at least one tenth of all voting members, or request of the auditors.
§ 10  The General Assembly
  • All members must be invited in writing at least two weeks in advance with the agenda.
  • Motions must be submitted in writing to the board at least one week in advance.
  • The general assembly is quorate when all members have been duly invited and more than 50% of voting members are present or connected via video conference.
  • Resolutions require a simple majority of valid votes cast.
  • The board chairs the general assembly.
§ 11  Responsibilities of the General Assembly
  • Acceptance and approval of the annual report and financial statements.
  • Approval of the budget.
  • Election and removal of board members and auditors.
  • Approval of transactions between auditors and the association.
  • Discharge of the board.
  • Setting of membership fees and joining fees.
  • Conferment and revocation of honorary membership.
  • Amendment of the statutes and dissolution of the association.
§ 12  The Board
  • The board consists of: the Chairman (Obmann), the Deputy Chairman, the Secretary, the Deputy Secretary, the Treasurer, and the Deputy Treasurer.
  • Board members are elected by the general assembly for a term of two years. Re-election is possible.
  • The board meets as required. It is quorate when all members have been duly invited and at least two are present.
  • Resolutions require a simple majority; in case of a tie the Chairman has the casting vote.
§ 13  Responsibilities of the Board
  • Management of the association — all tasks not assigned to another body by the statutes.
  • Preparation of annual budgets and financial statements.
  • Preparation and convening of general assemblies.
  • Administration of association assets.
  • Admission and exclusion of members.
  • Maintenance of the membership list.
  • Hiring and dismissal of association employees.
  • Notifying the competent tax authority of any statute changes affecting tax privileges within one month.
§ 14  Auditors
  • Two auditors are elected by the general assembly for a term of two years.
  • Auditors must audit the financial management of the association and report to the general assembly.
  • Auditors may not be members of the board.
§ 15  Arbitration Tribunal
  • All disputes arising from the association relationship are decided by the internal arbitration tribunal.
  • The arbitration tribunal consists of three ordinary members, two nominated by each party and one elected as chairman by the nominated arbitrators.
  • The arbitration tribunal decides by simple majority after hearing both parties. Its decisions are final within the association.
§ 16  Arbitration Procedure
  • The arbitration tribunal is composed of two ordinary association members. Each party nominates one arbitrator in writing. The arbitrators elect a second ordinary member as chairman within 14 days.
  • Arbitrators may not belong to any body whose activities are the subject of the dispute.
  • The arbitration tribunal decides in accordance with equity and good conscience. Its decisions are final within the association.
§ 17  Data Protection
  • Data protection provisions must be strictly observed.
  • By joining, each member gives irrevocable consent that their personal data (name, date of birth, profession, function, training) may be processed within the association for administrative, accounting, and information purposes.
§ 18  Dissolution of the Association
  • In the event of voluntary or official dissolution, or lapse of the association's purpose, the remaining assets after settlement of liabilities shall be used for charitable purposes within the meaning of §§ 34 ff BAO.
  • Assets must be transferred to a non-profit organisation with a purpose corresponding to or closely related to that of Beyond Thinking, as nominated by the last general assembly.
Registered: 12 November 2020 · BH Liezen GZ 2.1.Vr 42/2020 · ZVR-Zahl 1647187653
Lupitsch 123 · 8992 Altaussee · Austria · contact@beyondthinking.net

EU Law Privacy & GDPR

Regulation (EU) 2016/679 — GDPR / DSGVO
What We Collect

Only data you voluntarily submit via the contact form: name, email, and message. No data is stored on our servers — submissions go directly to your email client via mailto. We collect no IP addresses, device data, or behavioural information.

No Tracking

This website uses no cookies, no analytics (Google Analytics, Matomo etc.), no advertising pixels, and no third-party tracking of any kind.

Legal Basis (Art. 6 GDPR)

Processing is based on your consent (Art. 6(1)(a)) and to respond to your enquiry (Art. 6(1)(b)). Data is never sold or shared with third parties.

Your Rights (Art. 15–22 GDPR)
  • Access, rectification, erasure, restriction, portability
  • Right to object & right against automated decisions
  • Complaint to: Datenschutzbehörde (dsb.gv.at)
Data Controller

Beyond Thinking NGO · ZVR 1647187653
contact@beyondthinking.net

Last updated: June 2026 · Full policy available on request.

Compliance EU AI Act

Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 — in force since 1 Aug 2024
What It Is

The EU AI Act is the world's first comprehensive legal framework for artificial intelligence, establishing a risk-based classification: unacceptable risk (prohibited), high risk (strict obligations), limited risk (transparency), and minimal risk (free to use).

Our Risk Level

Beyond Thinking NGO operates exclusively at minimal and limited risk levels. We do not deploy high-risk AI systems (Annex III) or any prohibited AI practice (Art. 5).

  • No biometric identification systems
  • No automated decision-making affecting rights
  • No subliminal manipulation or social scoring
Our Commitments
  • AI Literacy (Art. 4) — we actively promote AI literacy as part of our mission
  • Transparency (Art. 50) — AI-assisted outputs are clearly disclosed
  • Human Oversight — all AI outputs reviewed and validated by our team
  • Fundamental Rights — non-discrimination, privacy, dignity respected in all AI use
Compliance Status
  • ✓ Feb 2025 — Prohibited practices & AI literacy: compliant
  • ✓ Aug 2025 — GPAI obligations: not applicable
  • ✓ Aug 2026 — High-risk AI: not applicable

Questions: contact@beyondthinking.net
